title: Wolff-Parkinson-White Syndrome
oneLiner: Congenital accessory atrioventricular pathway causing pre-excitation (delta wave) predisposing to paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardias and sudden cardiac death.

sections.Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) Syndrome:
**One-liner:** Congenital accessory atrioventricular pathway causing pre-excitation (delta wave) predisposing to paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardias and sudden cardiac death.

---
sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic Vignette:**  
A 22-year-old otherwise healthy female presents to ED with sudden-onset palpitations that started while studying. She feels her heart “racing” and is dizzy but not syncopal. No chest pain, no dyspnoea. She has had similar episodes before—they come on suddenly and stop abruptly. Vital signs: HR 220 bpm, BP 110/70, SpO₂ 98%. ECG shows a narrow-complex tachycardia (QRS < 120 ms) with no visible P waves.

**Dissect the vignette:**  
- *Young, healthy patient* → congenital problem (structural heart disease unlikely)  
- *Sudden onset/offset* → re-entrant tachycardia (AVNRT or AVRT)  
- *Heart rate > 200* → typical of pre-excited tachyarrhythmias; lower rates not common in WPW  
- *Dizziness but no syncope* → reasonable cerebral perfusion despite high rate; signs of instability demand immediate action  
- *Narrow-complex tachycardia without P waves* → orthodromic AVRT (commonest WPW tachycardia)  
- *Previous episodes* → suggests underlying substrate (accessory pathway)

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**  
- **Syncope on presentation** (especially in atrial fibrillation with pre-excitation – risk of ventricular fibrillation)  
- **Wide-complex tachycardia** (antidromic AVRT or atrial fibrillation conducted anterogradely down the accessory pathway) – mimics ventricular tachycardia  
- **Asymptomatic pre-excitation** – delta wave found incidentally on routine ECG (risk of sudden death in certain patients, especially those with short refractory period)

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**  
WPW is a conduction disorder where an extra muscle bridge (accessory pathway) connects the atria to the ventricles, bypassing the normal AV node. This “short circuit” allows the ventricles to be activated early (pre-excitation) and creates a circus movement for re-entry tachycardias.

**Why it matters:**  
- **Frequency:** 0.1–0.3% of the general population; 1–3 per 1000.  
- **Stakes:** Risk of sudden cardiac death (SCD) – rare but real (≈ 1–3 per 1000 patient-years). Most deaths are due to pre-excited atrial fibrillation degenerating to VF.  
- **Common pitfalls:** Mistaking orthodromic AVRT for AVNRT (management differs for long-term ablation strategy). Giving AV nodal blockers in pre-excited atrial fibrillation can precipitate VF. Missing the diagnosis in a young patient with syncope.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
**The story of pre-excitation:**  
During cardiac embryogenesis, the atria and ventricles are normally separated by the fibrous annulus, with only the AV node conducting. In WPW, small remnants of conducting tissue persist – the **bundle of Kent**. This pathway has different electrophysiological properties than the AV node: it **conducts faster** and has **no or little decremental conduction** (i.e., it doesn’t slow down as heart rate increases, unlike the AV node).

**How the delta wave appears:**  
In sinus rhythm, the impulse travels down the accessory pathway faster than through the AV node, activating a segment of ventricular myocardium *early*. This early depolarisation spreads slowly from the ventricular insertion site (like a wavefront from a point) creating a **slurred upstroke** (delta wave) on the QRS. The rest of the ventricle then activates normally via the His-Purkinje system, producing a widened QRS. Because part of the ventricle is excited early, the PR interval is **short** (the impulse arrives at the ventricle before the normal conduction has even left the AV node).

**Why tachycardias occur:**  
The presence of two parallel pathways (AV node and accessory pathway) creates a potential **re-entry circuit**. The most common tachycardia is **orthodromic AVRT**: the impulse goes down the AV node (normal direction) and back up the accessory pathway (retrograde). Because ventricular activation is via the His-Purkinje system, the QRS is **narrow** (no pre-excitation). P waves are hidden in the ST-T segment.  
- If the impulse travels *down* the accessory pathway and *up* the AV node, the QRS is **wide** (antidromic AVRT) – rarer.  
- In **atrial fibrillation** with pre-excitation, multiple fibrillatory waves can be conducted rapidly down the accessory pathway (which has a short refractory period) → very fast ventricular rates → risk of VF.

**Connect to pattern recognition cues:**  
- *Sudden onset/offset* → re-entry circuit can be initiated by a PAC (premature atrial contraction) or PVC  
- *Narrow complex tachycardia* → orthodromic AVRT (most common)  
- *Wide complex tachycardia* → antidromic or pre-excited AF  
- *Delta wave present in sinus rhythm* → pre-excitation

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Classic Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-------------------|
| **Idiopathic** | Congenital accessory pathway (most common) | Young, healthy individual; incidental ECG finding |
| **Structural heart disease** | Ebstein anomaly (50% have accessory pathways) | Cyanotic congenital heart disease with tricuspid valve displacement |
| **Genetic** | HCM (hypertrophic cardiomyopathy) – rarely associated | Young athlete with sudden syncope; family history of SCD |
| **Acquired** | Post-cardiac surgery (especially correction of Ebstein) | Post-operative ECG shows delta wave |
| **Triggers for tachycardia** | Stress, caffeine, alcohol, fever, dehydration | Palpitations during exam or after party |

**Most common cause:** Idiopathic congenital accessory pathway (no other structural disease).

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:**  
- ECG in sinus rhythm: delta wave (slurred upstroke), short PR < 110 ms (in adults), wide QRS ≥ 110 ms.  
- Tachycardia: orthodromic AVRT = narrow complex, rate ~200–280, P waves inverted and may be seen after QRS. Antidromic = wide complex (difficult to distinguish from VT).  
- **Single finding that confirms WPW:** Delta wave + clinical history of palpitations.  
- **Single finding that rules out (in ambiguous wide complex tachycardia):** presence of AV dissociation (seen in VT).

**Key tests:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| **12-lead ECG (baseline)** | Delta wave, short PR, wide QRS | Screen for pre-excitation | Delta wave direction can localise pathway (e.g., left lateral vs posteroseptal) |
| **12-lead during tachycardia** | Orthodromic: narrow complex; Antidromic: wide complex | Confirm type of SVT | If pre-excited AF: irregular wide-complex tachycardia with rates > 200 |
| **Holter monitor** | Episodes of SVT with delta wave | Document sporadic symptoms | Shows onset/offset; helps correlate symptoms |
| **Echocardiogram** | No structural heart disease (in typical WPW) | Rule out Ebstein, HCM | Normal echo = low risk unless high-risk pathway |
| **Exercise stress test** | May block accessory pathway at high heart rates | Assess risk of SCD (loss of delta wave?) | If pre-excitation disappears at high HR, pathway may have long refractory period (lower risk) |
| **Electrophysiology study (EPS)** | Intracardiac signals; can measure refractory period of pathway | Gold standard for diagnosis and ablation | Shortest pre-excited RR interval ≤ 250 ms during AF → high risk of sudden death |

**Pattern recognition of delta wave localisation:**  
- Positive delta in inferior leads (II, III, aVF) – likely left lateral  
- Negative delta in V1 – right-sided pathway  
- Positive in V1 – left-sided or posteroseptal

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour) – patient in tachycardia:**

- **Haemodynamically unstable** (BP < 90, syncope, chest pain, pulmonary oedema):  
  - **Synchronised DC cardioversion** – 50–100 J initially (biphasic).  
  - Why: Immediate termination of dangerous rhythm.

- **Stable narrow-complex tachycardia** (presumed orthodromic AVRT):  
  - **Vagal manoeuvres** first (Valsalva, carotid massage).  
  - If ineffective: **Adenosine** 6 mg IV rapid bolus (then 12 mg, then 18 mg if needed).  
    - Why: Adenosine blocks AV node, breaking the re-entry circuit.  
    - **Warning:** Transient asystole, bronchospasm (avoid in asthma).  
  - Alternative: **Verapamil** 5–10 mg IV (but avoid if wide-complex or unknown).

- **Stable wide-complex tachycardia – unclear if VT or antidromic AVRT:**  
  - If stable, try **adenosine** (may convert AVRT) – but be cautious; if unsure, treat as VT with **amiodarone** 150 mg IV over 10 min.  
  - Avoid verapamil – can cause haemodynamic collapse in VT.

- **Pre-excited atrial fibrillation** (irregular wide-complex tachycardia, HR often > 250):  
  - **Unstable** → immediate cardioversion.  
  - **Stable** → **Procainamide** 50 mg/min IV (max 17 mg/kg) or **ibutilide** 1 mg IV.  
  - What NOT to do: **DO NOT give AV nodal blockers** (adenosine, verapamil, diltiazem, beta-blockers, digoxin) – they can block the AV node, forcing more conduction down the accessory pathway → may precipitate VF.

**Ward (24–72 hours):**  
- **Continuous telemetry** for recurrent episodes.  
- **Echocardiogram** to exclude structural heart disease.  
- **Electrophysiology consultation** for consideration of **catheter ablation**.  
- **Discharge with safety netting:** educate patient about vagal manoeuvres, symptom diary, when to return.

**Long-term:**  
- **Catheter ablation** is first-line definitive treatment – success rate > 95% for most pathways, low complication rate (< 1-2% for AV block, tamponade).  
- **Medical therapy** (if ablation declined or contraindicated):  
  - Class IC antiarrhythmics (flecainide 100–200 mg daily) – for symptomatic SVT.  
  - Beta-blockers (atenolol 25–100 mg daily) – reduce triggers.  
  - **Avoid digoxin** (enhances accessory pathway conduction) and **verapamil** (risk of degeneration).  
- **Asymptomatic patients** with low-risk pathway (EPS-documented long refractory period) – may observe. Paediatric patients – risk is low but ablation is often offered due to lifestyle.

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | WPW (orthodromic AVRT) | Atrioventricular Nodal Re-entrant Tachycardia (AVNRT) | Ventricular Tachycardia (VT) | Atrial Fibrillation with Pre-excitation |
|---------|------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| **Pain / symptoms** | Palpitations sudden onset, dizziness, rarely syncope | Similar sudden onset, “neck pounding” (jugular cannon a waves) | Chest pain, syncope, shock – more haemodynamic compromise | Palpitations, severe dyspnoea, presyncope |
| **Exam** | Often stable; BP normal in orthodromic | Often stable | May have hypotension, Cannon A waves, variable BP | Rapid irregular pulse; may become unstable quickly |
| **ECG at baseline** | Delta wave, short PR, wide QRS | Normal (unless underlying heart) | Normal or evidence of ischaemia/cardiomyopathy | Delta wave during sinus rhythm (if intermittent) |
| **ECG during tachy** | Narrow complex (ortho); P waves after QRS (pseudo-R in aVR) | Narrow complex; P waves either absent or at the terminal end of QRS (pseudo-S in II) | Wide QRS (>120 ms); AV dissociation, fusion/capture beats | Irregular wide-complex; variable QRS morphology; rates > 250 |
| **Labs** | Normal | Normal | Troponin may be elevated (demand ischaemia) | Normal |
| **Key diff** | Delta wave at rest; adenosine converts | No delta wave at rest; adenosine converts (slows AV node) | Adenosine does not convert VT (may transiently slow) | Irregular rhythm + pre-excitation = WPW + AF |

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
- **Sudden cardiac death (SCD):**  
  - *Mechanism:* Pre-excited AF → VF.  
  - *Presenting signs:* Syncope, collapse, no pulse.  
  - *Key ECG:* Irregular wide-complex tachycardia with varying RR intervals (pre-excited AF).  
  - *What to do:* Immediate defibrillation (200 J). If witnessed arrest, start CPR.  
- **Tachycardiomyopathy:**  
  - *Who:* Persistent, unrecognised orthodromic AVRT (e.g., permanent junctional reciprocating tachycardia, PJRT – a rare form of WPW).  
  - *Presentation:* Heart failure symptoms (dyspnoea, oedema, reduced LVEF).  
  - *Key sign:* Narrow-complex tachycardia with inverted P waves in II, III, aVF, rate ~130–150.  
  - *What to do:* Echocardiogram, treat with ablation; LV function usually recovers fully.  
- **Pre-excited atrial fibrillation with degeneration to VF:**  
  - *Late presentation pattern:* Known WPW patient found collapsed; neighbours report previous “racing heart episodes never treated”.  
  - *What to do:* Despite successful VF termination, patient needs urgent EPS and ablation to prevent recurrence.  
- **Heart block (post-ablation):**  
  - *Complication of treatment:* Catheter ablation near the AV node can cause complete heart block requiring pacemaker.  
  - *Prevention:* Careful mapping of the pathway location relative to the AV node.

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

- **“Delta Wave = D for ‘Downstroke slurred’** – think of a triangle (delta) lifting the QRS start.  
- **“WPW: Wide QRS, P-wave? No, PR short, Wave of early activation”** – but that's not catchy.  
- **“Young person + narrow complex tachycardia = think of a ‘Short cut’ (accessory pathway)”**  
- **“Pre-excited AF – Don't Block the Shortcut!”** – Avoid AV nodal blockers.

**Clinical Pearls:**

1. **Delta wave can be subtle** – look for a notch at the beginning of the QRS (especially in V1–V3). If you see a narrow QRS but the PR is short, still consider intermittent pre-excitation.
2. **Localise the pathway by the delta wave polarity** – use the **Arruda** or **Milstein** algorithm (but remember: negative delta in V1 = right-sided; positive in V1 = left-sided).
3. **Never give adenosine in pre-excited AF** – you’ll regret it. Instead, use procainamide or cardioversion.
4. **Adenosine for orthodromic AVRT:** often the patient will break with a sinus tachycardia after transient asystole – warn them they’ll feel like they’ve been “hit by a bus”.
5. **Intermittent pre-excitation** (delta wave comes and goes on the same ECG) – usually implies a pathway with long refractory period and lower risk.
6. **Syncope in a patient with known WPW** is a red flag for VF risk – prompt EPS and ablation recommended.
7. **Asymptomatic patients with WPW** – need risk stratification (EP study if high-risk occupation, competitive athlete, or if delta wave is present during exercise). Loss of delta wave at high heart rates on treadmill test suggests a low-risk pathway.

**Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid:**

- Labeling a wide-complex tachycardia as VT when it's antidromic AVRT – adenosine may help differentiate (converts AVRT, not VT).
- Missing pre-excited AF because you think it's VT with some irregularity – look for “chaotic” wide QRS with variable morphology.
- Starting beta-blockers in a patient with WPW and AF – can worsen the situation.
- Forgetting that patients with WPW can also develop AVNRT – the accessory pathway may be an innocent bystander.

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**

- **Haemodynamic instability** (BP < 90 mmHg, altered consciousness, chest pain, pulmonary oedema) – call crash team, prepare for cardioversion.  
- **Pre-excited atrial fibrillation with HR > 250** – discuss with cardiology senior immediately; prepare for urgent cardioversion.  
- **Failed adenosine** (no conversion after 18 mg, or conversion to unstable rhythm) – senior review; consider alternative drugs or cardioversion.  
- **Recurrent SVT** despite vagal manoeuvres and adenosine – need EPS planning.  
- **After successful cardioversion**, if patient is known WPW and new neurological symptoms – suspect cardioembolic stroke (rare but possible).

**Specific thresholds:**

| Parameter | Threshold | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| **NEWS2** | ≥ 7 (or any persistent red score) | Escalate to medical emergency team |
| **Lactate** | > 4 mmol/L in the presence of tachyarrhythmia | Consider underlying ischaemia; senior review |
| **GCS** | < 15 (new onset) | Potential cerebral hypoperfusion; urgent re-evaluation |
| **Systolic BP** | < 90 mmHg with tachycardia | Immediate cardioversion if persistent |
| **QTc on ECG** | > 500 ms (if on antiarrhythmic drugs) | Stop drug; review EPS results |

**Final note:** WPW is a treatable cause of life-threatening arrhythmias in young patients. When you see a delta wave, your job is to risk-stratify and offer curative treatment. Don't let it become an “incidental finding” without a proper plan.

## Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) Syndrome

**One-liner:** Congenital accessory atrioventricular pathway causing pre-excitation (delta wave) predisposing to paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardias and sudden cardiac death.

---

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic Vignette:**  
A 22-year-old otherwise healthy female presents to ED with sudden-onset palpitations that started while studying. She feels her heart “racing” and is dizzy but not syncopal. No chest pain, no dyspnoea. She has had similar episodes before—they come on suddenly and stop abruptly. Vital signs: HR 220 bpm, BP 110/70, SpO₂ 98%. ECG shows a narrow-complex tachycardia (QRS < 120 ms) with no visible P waves.

**Dissect the vignette:**  
- *Young, healthy patient* → congenital problem (structural heart disease unlikely)  
- *Sudden onset/offset* → re-entrant tachycardia (AVNRT or AVRT)  
- *Heart rate > 200* → typical of pre-excited tachyarrhythmias; lower rates not common in WPW  
- *Dizziness but no syncope* → reasonable cerebral perfusion despite high rate; signs of instability demand immediate action  
- *Narrow-complex tachycardia without P waves* → orthodromic AVRT (commonest WPW tachycardia)  
- *Previous episodes* → suggests underlying substrate (accessory pathway)

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**  
- **Syncope on presentation** (especially in atrial fibrillation with pre-excitation – risk of ventricular fibrillation)  
- **Wide-complex tachycardia** (antidromic AVRT or atrial fibrillation conducted anterogradely down the accessory pathway) – mimics ventricular tachycardia  
- **Asymptomatic pre-excitation** – delta wave found incidentally on routine ECG (risk of sudden death in certain patients, especially those with short refractory period)

---

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:**  
WPW is a conduction disorder where an extra muscle bridge (accessory pathway) connects the atria to the ventricles, bypassing the normal AV node. This “short circuit” allows the ventricles to be activated early (pre-excitation) and creates a circus movement for re-entry tachycardias.

**Why it matters:**  
- **Frequency:** 0.1–0.3% of the general population; 1–3 per 1000.  
- **Stakes:** Risk of sudden cardiac death (SCD) – rare but real (≈ 1–3 per 1000 patient-years). Most deaths are due to pre-excited atrial fibrillation degenerating to VF.  
- **Common pitfalls:** Mistaking orthodromic AVRT for AVNRT (management differs for long-term ablation strategy). Giving AV nodal blockers in pre-excited atrial fibrillation can precipitate VF. Missing the diagnosis in a young patient with syncope.

---

### 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings

**The story of pre-excitation:**  
During cardiac embryogenesis, the atria and ventricles are normally separated by the fibrous annulus, with only the AV node conducting. In WPW, small remnants of conducting tissue persist – the **bundle of Kent**. This pathway has different electrophysiological properties than the AV node: it **conducts faster** and has **no or little decremental conduction** (i.e., it doesn’t slow down as heart rate increases, unlike the AV node).

**How the delta wave appears:**  
In sinus rhythm, the impulse travels down the accessory pathway faster than through the AV node, activating a segment of ventricular myocardium *early*. This early depolarisation spreads slowly from the ventricular insertion site (like a wavefront from a point) creating a **slurred upstroke** (delta wave) on the QRS. The rest of the ventricle then activates normally via the His-Purkinje system, producing a widened QRS. Because part of the ventricle is excited early, the PR interval is **short** (the impulse arrives at the ventricle before the normal conduction has even left the AV node).

**Why tachycardias occur:**  
The presence of two parallel pathways (AV node and accessory pathway) creates a potential **re-entry circuit**. The most common tachycardia is **orthodromic AVRT**: the impulse goes down the AV node (normal direction) and back up the accessory pathway (retrograde). Because ventricular activation is via the His-Purkinje system, the QRS is **narrow** (no pre-excitation). P waves are hidden in the ST-T segment.  
- If the impulse travels *down* the accessory pathway and *up* the AV node, the QRS is **wide** (antidromic AVRT) – rarer.  
- In **atrial fibrillation** with pre-excitation, multiple fibrillatory waves can be conducted rapidly down the accessory pathway (which has a short refractory period) → very fast ventricular rates → risk of VF.

**Connect to pattern recognition cues:**  
- *Sudden onset/offset* → re-entry circuit can be initiated by a PAC (premature atrial contraction) or PVC  
- *Narrow complex tachycardia* → orthodromic AVRT (most common)  
- *Wide complex tachycardia* → antidromic or pre-excited AF  
- *Delta wave present in sinus rhythm* → pre-excitation

---

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause | Classic Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-------------------|
| **Idiopathic** | Congenital accessory pathway (most common) | Young, healthy individual; incidental ECG finding |
| **Structural heart disease** | Ebstein anomaly (50% have accessory pathways) | Cyanotic congenital heart disease with tricuspid valve displacement |
| **Genetic** | HCM (hypertrophic cardiomyopathy) – rarely associated | Young athlete with sudden syncope; family history of SCD |
| **Acquired** | Post-cardiac surgery (especially correction of Ebstein) | Post-operative ECG shows delta wave |
| **Triggers for tachycardia** | Stress, caffeine, alcohol, fever, dehydration | Palpitations during exam or after party |

**Most common cause:** Idiopathic congenital accessory pathway (no other structural disease).

---

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria:**  
- ECG in sinus rhythm: delta wave (slurred upstroke), short PR < 110 ms (in adults), wide QRS ≥ 110 ms.  
- Tachycardia: orthodromic AVRT = narrow complex, rate ~200–280, P waves inverted and may be seen after QRS. Antidromic = wide complex (difficult to distinguish from VT).  
- **Single finding that confirms WPW:** Delta wave + clinical history of palpitations.  
- **Single finding that rules out (in ambiguous wide complex tachycardia):** presence of AV dissociation (seen in VT).

**Key tests:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| **12-lead ECG (baseline)** | Delta wave, short PR, wide QRS | Screen for pre-excitation | Delta wave direction can localise pathway (e.g., left lateral vs posteroseptal) |
| **12-lead during tachycardia** | Orthodromic: narrow complex; Antidromic: wide complex | Confirm type of SVT | If pre-excited AF: irregular wide-complex tachycardia with rates > 200 |
| **Holter monitor** | Episodes of SVT with delta wave | Document sporadic symptoms | Shows onset/offset; helps correlate symptoms |
| **Echocardiogram** | No structural heart disease (in typical WPW) | Rule out Ebstein, HCM | Normal echo = low risk unless high-risk pathway |
| **Exercise stress test** | May block accessory pathway at high heart rates | Assess risk of SCD (loss of delta wave?) | If pre-excitation disappears at high HR, pathway may have long refractory period (lower risk) |
| **Electrophysiology study (EPS)** | Intracardiac signals; can measure refractory period of pathway | Gold standard for diagnosis and ablation | Shortest pre-excited RR interval ≤ 250 ms during AF → high risk of sudden death |

- Positive delta in inferior leads (II, III, aVF) – likely left lateral  
- Negative delta in V1 – right-sided pathway  
- Positive in V1 – left-sided or posteroseptal

---

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour) – patient in tachycardia:**

- **Haemodynamically unstable** (BP < 90, syncope, chest pain, pulmonary oedema):  
  - **Synchronised DC cardioversion** – 50–100 J initially (biphasic).  
  - Why: Immediate termination of dangerous rhythm.

- **Stable narrow-complex tachycardia** (presumed orthodromic AVRT):  
  - **Vagal manoeuvres** first (Valsalva, carotid massage).  
  - If ineffective: **Adenosine** 6 mg IV rapid bolus (then 12 mg, then 18 mg if needed).  
    - Why: Adenosine blocks AV node, breaking the re-entry circuit.  
    - **Warning:** Transient asystole, bronchospasm (avoid in asthma).  
  - Alternative: **Verapamil** 5–10 mg IV (but avoid if wide-complex or unknown).

- **Stable wide-complex tachycardia – unclear if VT or antidromic AVRT:**  
  - If stable, try **adenosine** (may convert AVRT) – but be cautious; if unsure, treat as VT with **amiodarone** 150 mg IV over 10 min.  
  - Avoid verapamil – can cause haemodynamic collapse in VT.

- **Pre-excited atrial fibrillation** (irregular wide-complex tachycardia, HR often > 250):  
  - **Unstable** → immediate cardioversion.  
  - **Stable** → **Procainamide** 50 mg/min IV (max 17 mg/kg) or **ibutilide** 1 mg IV.  
  - What NOT to do: **DO NOT give AV nodal blockers** (adenosine, verapamil, diltiazem, beta-blockers, digoxin) – they can block the AV node, forcing more conduction down the accessory pathway → may precipitate VF.

**Ward (24–72 hours):**  
- **Continuous telemetry** for recurrent episodes.  
- **Echocardiogram** to exclude structural heart disease.  
- **Electrophysiology consultation** for consideration of **catheter ablation**.  
- **Discharge with safety netting:** educate patient about vagal manoeuvres, symptom diary, when to return.

**Long-term:**  
- **Catheter ablation** is first-line definitive treatment – success rate > 95% for most pathways, low complication rate (< 1-2% for AV block, tamponade).  
- **Medical therapy** (if ablation declined or contraindicated):  
  - Class IC antiarrhythmics (flecainide 100–200 mg daily) – for symptomatic SVT.  
  - Beta-blockers (atenolol 25–100 mg daily) – reduce triggers.  
  - **Avoid digoxin** (enhances accessory pathway conduction) and **verapamil** (risk of degeneration).  
- **Asymptomatic patients** with low-risk pathway (EPS-documented long refractory period) – may observe. Paediatric patients – risk is low but ablation is often offered due to lifestyle.

---

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | WPW (orthodromic AVRT) | Atrioventricular Nodal Re-entrant Tachycardia (AVNRT) | Ventricular Tachycardia (VT) | Atrial Fibrillation with Pre-excitation |
|---------|------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| **Pain / symptoms** | Palpitations sudden onset, dizziness, rarely syncope | Similar sudden onset, “neck pounding” (jugular cannon a waves) | Chest pain, syncope, shock – more haemodynamic compromise | Palpitations, severe dyspnoea, presyncope |
| **Exam** | Often stable; BP normal in orthodromic | Often stable | May have hypotension, Cannon A waves, variable BP | Rapid irregular pulse; may become unstable quickly |
| **ECG at baseline** | Delta wave, short PR, wide QRS | Normal (unless underlying heart) | Normal or evidence of ischaemia/cardiomyopathy | Delta wave during sinus rhythm (if intermittent) |
| **ECG during tachy** | Narrow complex (ortho); P waves after QRS (pseudo-R in aVR) | Narrow complex; P waves either absent or at the terminal end of QRS (pseudo-S in II) | Wide QRS (>120 ms); AV dissociation, fusion/capture beats | Irregular wide-complex; variable QRS morphology; rates > 250 |
| **Labs** | Normal | Normal | Troponin may be elevated (demand ischaemia) | Normal |
| **Key diff** | Delta wave at rest; adenosine converts | No delta wave at rest; adenosine converts (slows AV node) | Adenosine does not convert VT (may transiently slow) | Irregular rhythm + pre-excitation = WPW + AF |

---

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

- **Sudden cardiac death (SCD):**  
  - *Mechanism:* Pre-excited AF → VF.  
  - *Presenting signs:* Syncope, collapse, no pulse.  
  - *Key ECG:* Irregular wide-complex tachycardia with varying RR intervals (pre-excited AF).  
  - *What to do:* Immediate defibrillation (200 J). If witnessed arrest, start CPR.  
- **Tachycardiomyopathy:**  
  - *Who:* Persistent, unrecognised orthodromic AVRT (e.g., permanent junctional reciprocating tachycardia, PJRT – a rare form of WPW).  
  - *Presentation:* Heart failure symptoms (dyspnoea, oedema, reduced LVEF).  
  - *Key sign:* Narrow-complex tachycardia with inverted P waves in II, III, aVF, rate ~130–150.  
  - *What to do:* Echocardiogram, treat with ablation; LV function usually recovers fully.  
- **Pre-excited atrial fibrillation with degeneration to VF:**  
  - *Late presentation pattern:* Known WPW patient found collapsed; neighbours report previous “racing heart episodes never treated”.  
  - *What to do:* Despite successful VF termination, patient needs urgent EPS and ablation to prevent recurrence.  
- **Heart block (post-ablation):**  
  - *Complication of treatment:* Catheter ablation near the AV node can cause complete heart block requiring pacemaker.  
  - *Prevention:* Careful mapping of the pathway location relative to the AV node.

---

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

- **“Delta Wave = D for ‘Downstroke slurred’** – think of a triangle (delta) lifting the QRS start.  
- **“WPW: Wide QRS, P-wave? No, PR short, Wave of early activation”** – but that's not catchy.  
- **“Young person + narrow complex tachycardia = think of a ‘Short cut’ (accessory pathway)”**  
- **“Pre-excited AF – Don't Block the Shortcut!”** – Avoid AV nodal blockers.

**Clinical Pearls:**

1. **Delta wave can be subtle** – look for a notch at the beginning of the QRS (especially in V1–V3). If you see a narrow QRS but the PR is short, still consider intermittent pre-excitation.
2. **Localise the pathway by the delta wave polarity** – use the **Arruda** or **Milstein** algorithm (but remember: negative delta in V1 = right-sided; positive in V1 = left-sided).
3. **Never give adenosine in pre-excited AF** – you’ll regret it. Instead, use procainamide or cardioversion.
4. **Adenosine for orthodromic AVRT:** often the patient will break with a sinus tachycardia after transient asystole – warn them they’ll feel like they’ve been “hit by a bus”.
5. **Intermittent pre-excitation** (delta wave comes and goes on the same ECG) – usually implies a pathway with long refractory period and lower risk.
6. **Syncope in a patient with known WPW** is a red flag for VF risk – prompt EPS and ablation recommended.
7. **Asymptomatic patients with WPW** – need risk stratification (EP study if high-risk occupation, competitive athlete, or if delta wave is present during exercise). Loss of delta wave at high heart rates on treadmill test suggests a low-risk pathway.

**Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid:**

- Labeling a wide-complex tachycardia as VT when it's antidromic AVRT – adenosine may help differentiate (converts AVRT, not VT).
- Missing pre-excited AF because you think it's VT with some irregularity – look for “chaotic” wide QRS with variable morphology.
- Starting beta-blockers in a patient with WPW and AF – can worsen the situation.
- Forgetting that patients with WPW can also develop AVNRT – the accessory pathway may be an innocent bystander.

---

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**

- **Haemodynamic instability** (BP < 90 mmHg, altered consciousness, chest pain, pulmonary oedema) – call crash team, prepare for cardioversion.  
- **Pre-excited atrial fibrillation with HR > 250** – discuss with cardiology senior immediately; prepare for urgent cardioversion.  
- **Failed adenosine** (no conversion after 18 mg, or conversion to unstable rhythm) – senior review; consider alternative drugs or cardioversion.  
- **Recurrent SVT** despite vagal manoeuvres and adenosine – need EPS planning.  
- **After successful cardioversion**, if patient is known WPW and new neurological symptoms – suspect cardioembolic stroke (rare but possible).

**Specific thresholds:**

| Parameter | Threshold | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| **NEWS2** | ≥ 7 (or any persistent red score) | Escalate to medical emergency team |
| **Lactate** | > 4 mmol/L in the presence of tachyarrhythmia | Consider underlying ischaemia; senior review |
| **GCS** | < 15 (new onset) | Potential cerebral hypoperfusion; urgent re-evaluation |
| **Systolic BP** | < 90 mmHg with tachycardia | Immediate cardioversion if persistent |
| **QTc on ECG** | > 500 ms (if on antiarrhythmic drugs) | Stop drug; review EPS results |

**Final note:** WPW is a treatable cause of life-threatening arrhythmias in young patients. When you see a delta wave, your job is to risk-stratify and offer curative treatment. Don't let it become an “incidental finding” without a proper plan.
